item.title: Finally, a team!
item.description: Last time I began to describe what makes a team and promised to finish. There are 3 additional requirements to make a successful team, but I want to review briefly the first two. The first requirement for the most successful team is picking the best people. It goes without saying that mediocre talent does not create all-star results. Don’t be afraid to pick your best people, unless you know that one or more are so unprofessional that they will be disruptive. Even if this group hasn’t worked

item.title: Pursuing the Elusive Team
item.description: What is a “team”? When you hear the word “team” you likely imagine a sports team. And of course professional sports are excellent examples of highly organized and interdependent teams. But, what makes them that way and why does it matter in business or research? The short answer: A fully functional team is always more efficient, effective and dynamic than any other group. A quick quiz – identify the team from the following examples: A group of interested people volunteering together to

item.title: Why I chose MSI
item.description: Hello, my name is Dr. David Kiewlich and I wanted introduce myself and tell you about MSI and why I am excited to be part of this team. Hopefully this will encourage you to join us as well! I have always been an entrepreneur, although I didn’t know what that meant until recently. I think the best way to describe an entrepreneur would be someone who is never satisfied with what is. When I think of the world, I don’t envision what is, but what can and should be and how I can make that happen. My
